project.title: Nitrosylation-mediated regulation of MEK-ERK signaling in the NRAS-mutant melanoma
project.projectDescription: NRAS activating mutations occur in 15-25% of melanoma patients. However, this subtype is more aggressive and refractory to current treatment modalities, including targeted MEK inhibitors, immunotherapies, and the recently emerging RAS inhibitors. Accordingly, identifying novel therapeutic targets and designing novel antimelanoma strategies is of utmost clinical relevance. Melanomas often harbor hyperactive nitric oxide synthases (NOS) which correlates strongly with poor prognosis. NOS is also known to be activated by ERK signaling. The nitric oxide so produced, induces nitrosylation, a post-translational modification, which dysregulates various kinases and phosphatases. We sensitized NRAS-mutant melanomas to targeted MEK inhibitors by inhibiting nitrosylation. This outcome revealed a strong correlation between global de-nitrosylation and downregulation of MEK-ERK cascade with a concomitant de-nitrosylation of NRAS and other small GTPases like Rho and Rac, dual specificity phosphatases, and ribosomal S6 kinase. In vivo, we observed extreme regression of NRAS-mutant melanoma tumors generated in an immunocompetent mouse model in response to a sequential nitrosylation inhibition and MEK inhibition. In addition to MEK-ERK downregulation, this strategy activated the CD8+ T cells in the tumor microenvironment. We conclude that while nitrosylation hyperactivates the RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK cascade, it also suppresses anti-melanoma immune response, bolstering progression and MEKi resistance. The underlying mechanism(s) will be crucial in identifying novel therapeutic vulnerabilities against aggressive, currently incurable NRAS-mutant melanomas with potential clinical application in other melanoma subtypes.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: The nitrosylated proteins were detected using the previously established Biotin Switch Technique with minor modifications. Cells were lysed in HENS buffer (Cat. No. 90106, ThermoFisher Scientific), supplemented with a protease cocktail inhibitor (Cat. 78429, ThermoFisher Scientific). For efficient blocking of free thiols, the pH of HENS should be 6-6.5. The protein concentrations were estimated by BCA protein estimation kit (Cat. No. 23225, ThermoFisher Scientific). Free thiols (-SH groups) were chemically blocked with 20 mM N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) (Cat. No. E1271, Millipore-Sigma) for 30 minutes at 50 °C, and excess NEM was removed by acetone precipitation(Cat. No. 67-64-1, Sigma). After resuspending the pellet in HENS, the S-nitrosylated (-SNO) thiols on Cysteines were reduced to -SH group using 1 mM ascorbate (Cat. No. PHR1279, Sigma), and labelled with biotin using 0.8 mM Biotin-HPDP (Cat. No. 16459, Cayman Chemicals) by incubation at room temperature for 2 hours in the dark. Excess labeling reagents were removed by Acetone precipitation. The protein pellets were washed twice with aqueous 70% acetone, dried for ~5 minutes at room temperature, and resuspended in HENS buffer. For electrophoretic analyses, 10-15 ug total biotin labelled lysate was used on pre-cast 4-20% SDS PAGE (Cat. 4561091, Bio-Rad, CA, USA). After transferring to the PVDF membranes (Cat. No. 162-0184) using standard procedures. As a positive control, nitrosylation was induced using chemical sources of nitric oxide; S-nitroso-L-glutathione or GSNO, formal name L-γ-glutamyl-S-nitroso-L-cysteinyl-glycine (Cat. 82240), and Diethylenetriamine or DETA-NONOate (Cat. 82120), both from Cayman Chemicals.   For identification of nitrosylated proteins, 400 - 800 µg of biotin-HPDP labeled protein was diluted with two volumes of neutralization buffer (20 mM HEPES, 100 mM NaCl, 1 mM EDTA, 0.5% v/v Triton X-100). Streptavidin magnetic beads (Cat. No. Z548C, Promega, WI, USA), or Streptavidin agarose resin (Cat. No. 20359, ThermoFisher Scientific) (30 µl bead slurry per 1 mg protein) were washed three times with 500 µl of neutralization buffer and added to the biotin-HPDP labeled protein. Incubation was carried out overnight at 4 0C. Beads were washed with neutralization buffer supplemented with 600 mM NaCl, followed by protein elution with elution buffers (Neutralization buffer + 5% β-mercaptoethanol), boiled on a heat block for 5-10 minutes. For mass spectrometry-based identification of the nitrosylated proteome (nitrosylome), the YUDOSO, YUTICA, and YUGASP cells were treated with 100 µM GSNO for 4-6 hours followed by BST to label the nitrosylated cysteines with biotin. Biotinylated proteins were recovered using neutravidin beads (Pierce), followed by trypsin digestion and peptide desalting. Cleaned peptides were labeled with TMT10plex reagents with the following channel layout: 3 YUDOSO-Control samples were labeled with 126C/127C/128C. 3 YUDOSO-GSNO samples were labeled with 127N/128N/129N. Inhibition sample was labeled with 130C and a negative control sample with 131N. After label incorporation check (LC-MS/MS and spectral counting of TMT-labeled versus underivatized peptides), all samples were combined and another neutravidin enrichment was performed to recover biotinylated peptides. The flowthrough peptides were separated with high-pH reversed phase offline fractionation cartridges into 4 fractions for protein identification. A nanoflow ultra high performance liquid chromatograph interfaced with an electrospray bench top orbitrap mass spectrometer (RSLCnano and Q Exactive HF-X, Thermo, San Jose, CA) was used for liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry peptide sequencing experiments.  The sample was first loaded onto a pre-column (C18 PepMap100, 100 µm ID x 2 cm length packed with 5 µm particle size, 100 Å pore size) and washed for 8 minutes with aqueous 2% acetonitrile with 0.1% formic acid.  The trapped peptides were eluted onto the analytical column, (C18 PepMap100, 75 µm ID x 25 cm length packed with 2 µm particles, 100 Å pore size, Thermo, San Jose, CA).  The 120-minute gradient was programmed as: 95% solvent A (2% acetonitrile + 0.1% formic acid) for 8 minutes, solvent B (aqueous 90% acetonitrile + 0.1% formic acid) from 5% to 38.5% in 90 minutes, then solvent B from 50% to 90% B in 7 minutes and held at 90% for 5 minutes, followed by solvent B from 90% to 5% in 1 minute and re-equilibration for 10 minutes.  The flow rate was 300 nl/min. Twenty tandem mass spectra were collected in a data-dependent manner following each survey scan. Spray voltage was 1900 V. Capillary temperature was 275°C. S-lens RF was set to 50. Normalized collision energy (NCE) was set at 27. The resolution settings were 60,000 and 45,000 for MS and MS/MS, respectively. The isolation window was 0.8 Th with 0.2 offset to capture the first two isotopes of each peptide for MS/MS.  Dynamic exclusion was set at 15 seconds.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: MaxQuant  (version 1.6.14.0) was used to identify proteins and quantify the TMT reporter ion intensities with default settings: Up to 2 missed trypsin cleavages were allowed. Carbamidomethyl cysteine was set as fixed modification. Biotin-HPDP and Methionine oxidation were set as variable modifications. Both Peptide Spectral Match and Protein FDR were set at 0.05. The human protein database was downloaded in March 2021 from Uniprot.org. Similar searches were performed in Mascot for this upload.

references.referenceLine: Srivastava J, Yadav VK, Jimenez RV, Phadatare PR, Inamdar NA, Young MM, Bacchiocchi A, Halaban R, Fang B, Pulido AM, Tsai KY, Smalley KSM, Koomen JM, Rodriguez PC, Premi S. Blocking Nitrosylation Induces Immunogenic Cell Death by Sensitizing NRAS-Mutant Melanoma to MEK Inhibitors. Cancer Res. 2025
